questions.question: How are express terms incorporated into a contract?

options.A: Only through written documents signed by both parties
options.B: By oral agreement, writing, or conduct — any means by which the parties communicate the terms they have agreed
options.C: Only through a formal deed executed before a notary
options.D: By statute, regardless of what the parties have agreed

questions.explanation: Express terms are those specifically agreed and articulated by the parties, whether orally, in writing, or by conduct. They represent the parties' actual bargain. There is no requirement that they be in writing (save where statute requires formality, e.g., contracts for land). However, written terms are easier to prove and less susceptible to dispute.

questions.question: What is the parol evidence rule?

options.A: A rule requiring all contracts to be in writing to be enforceable
options.B: A rule that extrinsic evidence (oral or documentary) cannot generally be admitted to add to, vary, or contradict the terms of a written contract
options.C: A rule that only written evidence is admissible in contract disputes
options.D: A rule preventing parties from giving evidence about pre-contractual negotiations

questions.explanation: The parol evidence rule provides that where the parties have reduced their agreement to writing, extrinsic evidence — whether oral statements, prior negotiations, or other documents outside the written contract — cannot be admitted to add to, vary, or contradict the written terms. The written document is treated as the complete and authoritative record of the parties' agreement.

questions.question: Which of the following is an established exception to the parol evidence rule?

options.A: Evidence of the parties' subjective intentions during negotiations
options.B: Evidence that the written contract does not represent the whole agreement — i.e., that additional oral terms were also agreed
options.C: Evidence that one party drove a harder bargain than the other
options.D: Evidence of market practice applicable to both parties

questions.explanation: The parol evidence rule does not prevent a party from showing that the written document was not intended to contain the whole of the agreement — that additional oral terms were also agreed alongside it (Couchman v Hill [1947]). Other exceptions include evidence of custom and usage, evidence of a condition precedent to the contract becoming operative, and evidence to establish a vitiating factor such as misrepresentation or mistake.

questions.question: What factors do courts consider when determining whether a pre-contractual statement is a term of the contract or merely a representation?

options.A: Whether the statement was made in writing, the date of the statement, and whether consideration was given
options.B: The importance attached to the statement, the relative knowledge of the parties, and whether the maker asked the other to verify the statement
options.C: Whether the statement was made by a qualified professional and witnessed by a third party
options.D: Whether the parties used the word 'term' when making the statement

questions.explanation: Courts consider: (1) the importance of the statement — if it induced the contract it is more likely to be a term (Bannerman v White [1861]); (2) the relative expertise/knowledge of the parties — a statement by the party with specialist knowledge is more likely to be a term (Dick Bentley Productions v Harold Smith Motors [1965] vs Oscar Chess v Williams [1957]); (3) the lapse of time between statement and contract; and (4) whether the maker invited verification, suggesting it was not a term (Ecay v Godfrey [1947]).

questions.question: In Oscar Chess Ltd v Williams [1957], the Court of Appeal held that the seller's statement about the car's year of manufacture was a representation, not a term, because:

options.A: The seller was a car dealer and should have known the true year
options.B: The seller was a private individual who honestly believed the statement and had no greater means of verification than the buyer
options.C: The statement was made orally and not incorporated into the written contract
options.D: The buyer had not relied on the statement when entering the contract

questions.explanation: In Oscar Chess v Williams [1957], Williams was a private person who sold a car believing (on the basis of the registration document) it was a 1948 model. Oscar Chess was a car dealer with superior means of verification. The Court of Appeal held the statement was a mere representation: Williams had no special knowledge or skill and could not have warranted the information he had no means of checking.

questions.question: In Dick Bentley Productions Ltd v Harold Smith (Motors) Ltd [1965], the Court of Appeal held the statement about mileage was a contractual term because:

options.A: The statement was made in writing and signed by both parties
options.B: The seller was a motor dealer with access to specialist knowledge and skill in assessing the car
options.C: The buyer had expressly insisted that the mileage was a condition of the contract
options.D: The buyer paid a higher price than market value in reliance on the statement

questions.explanation: In Dick Bentley Productions v Harold Smith (Motors) [1965], the car dealer (Harold Smith) had specialist knowledge and access to information about the car's history. The Court of Appeal held the statement about mileage was a warranty/term: a person who has, or claims to have, special knowledge or skill and makes a statement inducing the contract is more likely to be found to have given a contractual term, not merely a representation.

questions.question: What is a condition in the classification of contractual terms?

options.A: A term that is merely subsidiary to the main purpose of the contract
options.B: A term that is so fundamental that its breach entitles the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages
options.C: A term that operates as a precedent to the contract coming into existence
options.D: A term imposed by statute rather than agreed between the parties

questions.explanation: A condition is a term that goes to the root of the contract — it is so essential to the bargain that a breach of it entitles the innocent party to treat themselves as discharged from further performance and to claim damages. The classification as condition is determined by the parties' intentions (expressed or inferred) or by statute (e.g., implied conditions under the Sale of Goods Act 1893 / SGSSA 1980 / CRA 2022).

questions.question: What is a warranty in the context of contractual terms?

options.A: A fundamental term whose breach allows the innocent party to terminate
options.B: A term whose breach entitles the innocent party to damages only, but not to treat the contract as terminated
options.C: A written guarantee provided by a manufacturer about product quality
options.D: A term imposed by the Consumer Rights Act 2022

questions.explanation: A warranty is a term that is subsidiary to the main purpose of the contract. Breach of a warranty gives the innocent party the right to damages, but does NOT entitle them to terminate the contract. The party in breach has failed to perform the warranty, but the innocent party must continue to perform their own obligations and pursue a damages claim.

questions.question: What is an innominate (intermediate) term, as established in Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]?

options.A: A term that is automatically classified as a condition if agreed in writing
options.B: A term that cannot be classified in advance as either a condition or warranty — the consequences of breach depend on the nature and effect of the breach
options.C: A term that is intermediate because it is partly statutory and partly agreed
options.D: A term of implied intent where the parties have not expressly agreed on its classification

questions.explanation: The innominate (or intermediate) term category was established in Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]. Rather than classifying the term in advance as condition or warranty, the court looks at the actual consequence of the breach: if the breach deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit they were intended to receive under the contract, they may terminate; otherwise, they are limited to damages.

questions.question: In Poussard v Spiers and Pond [1876], a singer's failure to appear for the opening nights of an opera was held to be a breach of condition. This meant:

options.A: The theatre was entitled to damages only
options.B: The theatre was entitled to terminate the contract and engage a replacement singer
options.C: The singer could return and perform on later dates without consequence
options.D: The breach was a breach of warranty that had to be accepted

questions.explanation: In Poussard v Spiers and Pond [1876], the obligation to perform from the opening night was held to be a condition — it went to the root of the contract. The theatre management was therefore entitled to treat the contract as discharged when she failed to appear for the first few performances and were justified in engaging a permanent replacement.

questions.question: In Bettini v Gye [1876], a singer's failure to attend rehearsals for the first three days (out of a required six) was held to be a breach of warranty rather than condition. This meant:

options.A: The employer could terminate the contract and sue for loss of the whole engagement
options.B: The employer was entitled to damages for breach but could not terminate the contract
options.C: The contract was automatically terminated by statute
options.D: The employer had to accept performance despite the breach

questions.explanation: In Bettini v Gye [1876], the rehearsal clause did not go to the root of the contract — the main purpose was the singer's performance. The court held the rehearsal obligation was a warranty. The employer could claim damages for the lost rehearsals but was not entitled to repudiate the entire contract. This case is contrasted with Poussard v Spiers and Pond.

questions.question: What is the significance of the classification of a term as a condition (as opposed to an innominate term)?

options.A: Classification as a condition gives the innocent party an automatic right to terminate, regardless of the consequences of the actual breach
options.B: Classification as a condition limits the innocent party to damages only
options.C: Classification as a condition means the term is implied by statute
options.D: Classification as a condition means the term must be in writing to be enforced

questions.explanation: If a term is classified as a condition, any breach — however minor — gives the innocent party the right to terminate. This can create a risk of opportunistic termination. The innominate term approach (Hong Kong Fir) was developed to prevent parties from using a minor breach of a 'condition' as a pretext to escape an inconvenient contract. Certainty, however, is the justification for pre-classification as a condition.

questions.question: How is a term classified as a 'condition' in practice?

options.A: The court always classifies terms based on the severity of the breach actually suffered
options.B: By express agreement of the parties, by implication from the nature and purpose of the contract, or by statute
options.C: All written terms are conditions; all oral terms are warranties
options.D: A term is a condition only if both parties used that specific word in the contract

questions.explanation: A term may be classified as a condition by: (1) express agreement of the parties using the word 'condition' (though the court looks at substance over label); (2) implication from the nature and purpose of the contract — if performance of the term is essential to the bargain; or (3) by statute (e.g., the implied conditions of title and quality in the Sale of Goods Act 1893 and CRA 2022).

questions.question: In Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd [1974] (HL), the House of Lords was reluctant to hold that a clause described as a 'condition' actually entitled termination for any breach. What approach did the HL apply?

options.A: They held that the use of the word 'condition' was conclusive and allowed termination
options.B: They construed the contract as a whole and held the word 'condition' was not used in its strict technical sense — the consequences of treating every minor breach as entitling termination would be unreasonable
options.C: They applied the Hong Kong Fir approach to re-classify the term as innominate
options.D: They held that the clause was void for uncertainty

questions.explanation: In Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd [1974], the House of Lords held that despite the use of the word 'condition', the court should construe the contract as a whole. The commercial absurdity of allowing termination for any single failure out of 1,400 required visits supported the interpretation that 'condition' was not used in its strict legal sense. Context and commercial common sense prevail over labels.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the 'entire obligations' rule in relation to conditions?

options.A: A party must fully perform their obligations before becoming entitled to payment under a contract
options.B: A party may claim partial payment proportionate to the work performed
options.C: All contractual obligations must be performed simultaneously
options.D: Each party's obligations under a contract are independent and severable

questions.explanation: The entire obligations rule provides that where a contract is entire (as opposed to severable), a party must perform their obligations in full before becoming entitled to payment. In Cutter v Powell [1795], a sailor who died before completing the voyage was entitled to nothing. Partial performance of an entire contract does not entitle the performing party to payment, unless the other party accepts the partial performance.

questions.question: A buyer of goods states before contracting: 'If the goods are not first-quality cotton, I am not interested.' The seller agrees to supply. What is the legal effect of this statement?

options.A: It becomes a mere representation, not a term, because it was made before the contract
options.B: It becomes a condition of the contract — the buyer has indicated that compliance with this requirement is essential to the bargain
options.C: It becomes a warranty, giving the buyer damages only if the cotton is not first quality
options.D: It is too vague to be a contractual term

questions.explanation: In Bannerman v White [1861], the buyer said he would not even ask the price if sulphur had been used in treating the hops. The court held this elevated the term about sulphur use to a condition. Where a party makes clear that a particular quality is essential to the bargain and the other contracts on that basis, the relevant term is a condition whose breach entitles the innocent party to terminate.

questions.question: In the context of a charterparty, the term 'seaworthy' was considered in Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]. The Court of Appeal held this was an innominate term because:

options.A: The word 'seaworthy' was too vague to be a condition or warranty
options.B: The obligation of seaworthiness covered such a wide range of potential deficiencies — from trivial to catastrophic — that it could not be pre-classified; the remedy depended on the gravity of the breach
options.C: The charterers had waived their right to terminate by continuing to perform
options.D: Seaworthiness was expressly classified as a warranty in the charterparty

questions.explanation: In Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962], the Court of Appeal established the innominate term category. 'Seaworthiness' could be breached in an almost infinite number of ways — some trivial, some fundamental. It was therefore inappropriate to classify it in advance as condition or warranty. The appropriate remedy depended on whether the breach went to the root of the contract (allowing termination) or was less serious (limiting the remedy to damages).

questions.question: What is the effect of labelling a term as a 'warranty' in a contract?

options.A: The label is conclusive — the court will always treat it as a warranty regardless of its importance
options.B: The label is persuasive but not conclusive — courts will look at the substance and importance of the term, not just its label
options.C: The label is irrelevant — only statutory classification matters
options.D: The term is automatically a condition if it relates to the main subject matter

questions.explanation: Courts look at the substance of a term, not merely its label. A term described as a 'warranty' may in substance be a condition if it is so fundamental that its breach deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit. Conversely, 'condition' language may be interpreted as a mere warranty or innominate term if applied literally would produce absurd results (Schuler AG v Wickman [1974]).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between conditions, warranties and the Consumer Rights Act 2022 (Ireland)?

options.A: The CRA 2022 abolished the distinction between conditions and warranties in all contracts
options.B: The CRA 2022 implies statutory rights into consumer contracts with traders, including rights to reject (akin to conditions) and to repair, replacement or price reduction where rejection is not available
options.C: The CRA 2022 classifies all implied terms as innominate terms
options.D: The CRA 2022 only applies to warranties given voluntarily by manufacturers

questions.explanation: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 (Ireland) implies statutory terms into consumer contracts for goods, digital content and services. For goods, where those implied terms are breached, consumers have a short-term right to reject (analogous to a condition), followed by rights to repair or replacement, and ultimately a price reduction or final right to reject. The CRA 2022 structures these remedies in a tiered hierarchy rather than using the traditional condition/warranty terminology.

questions.question: In a contract for the sale of goods between businesses, which of the following implied terms is classified as a condition under the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 (Ireland)?

options.A: An implied term that the seller will deliver on the agreed date
options.B: An implied term that the goods are of merchantable quality
options.C: An implied term that the buyer will inspect the goods before acceptance
options.D: An implied term that the goods are sold at a fair market price

questions.explanation: Under the Sale of Goods Act 1893 (as amended by the SGSSA 1980), there is an implied condition (s14) that goods supplied in the course of a business are of merchantable quality (now 'satisfactory quality'). Being classified as a condition, breach entitles the buyer to reject the goods and terminate the contract, as well as claiming damages, subject to the rules on acceptance and affirmation.
